data.title: First Quantum: Market Euphoria Could Moderate Despite Robust Copper Price
data.description:
We Signal Tempered Enthusiasm For First Quantum Minerals Ltd.

This article is about the U.S. OTC-listed shares of First Quantum Minerals Ltd. (FQVLF). If we were investors, we would not add to our positions now, as market valuations appear somewhat overly optimistic and could be revised downwards in light of tighter production forecasts and hedging transactions that do not allow for the full benefit of rising commodity.

About First Quantum Minerals Ltd.

First Quantum Minerals Ltd., hereinafter referred to as “First Quantum” or “FQVLF”, is the following company, which Seeking Alpha describes in this screenshot:

First Quantum mines copper, some gold, and some nickel from its metal deposits. These include:

  • 80% of the Kansanshi copper/gold open-pit mine, 9 km northeast of Solwezi, Zambia.
  • 100% of the Sentinel copper open-pit mine, 60 km west of Manyama, Zambia.
  • 100% of the Enterprise Mine nickel open-pit mine, 70 km west of Manyama, Zambia.
  • 100% of the Guelb Moghrein copper/gold/iron ore open-pit mine, 246 km northeast of Nouakchott, Mauritania.
  • 100% of the Cayeli underground copper/zinc/gold/silver mine, 6.4 km southeast of Cayeli, Turkey.
  • 100% of the Cobre Las Cruces copper/gold tailings pile. Due to depleted reserves and completed tailings processing, the mine is currently in the care and maintenance (“C&M”) phase. The company will not further explore the mineral potential beyond the depleted deposits, but is in the process of selling this mineral property.
  • 100% of the Pyhasalmi copper/zinc/gold/silver/pyrite tailings, located 28 km west of Kiuruvesi, Finland.
  • 90% of the Cobre Panama copper/molybdenum/gold/silver open-pit mine, located 99 km southwest of Colón, Panama. On November 28, 2023, the company announced the cessation of all commercial production at Cobre Panama due to illegal blockades at the port, which disrupted the supply of electricity for the complex. The mine has since been put in the C&M phase. The government has approved the export of 120,000 tons of copper concentrate that has been stockpiled on-site since November 2023, as well as the recommissioning of the power plant.
  • 75.7% of the Ravensthorpe nickel/cobalt open pit mine, located 33 km southeast of Ravensthorpe, Western Australia. Due to low nickel prices and high operating costs, operations ceased in 2024. This included the shutdown of the high-pressure acid leaching facilities, the closure of the ore stockpiles processing and the resulting Ravensthorpe being brought under C&M.

The Company's Largest Exposure Is To Copper

First Quantum's results depend 85% on the copper price, a further 9% on the gold price, and the remaining 6% on nickel and other metals. This is evident from the operating and financial results for the third quarter of 2025 (hereinafter referred to as “ Q3-2025 Results”). It can be concluded that the impact of copper price fluctuations, expectations regarding future copper price developments, and the company's strategy—which aims partly to mitigate the negative effects of fluctuations and partly to capitalise on rising copper prices—are crucial to First Quantum's profitability, strategic production decisions, and balance sheet structure. These measures aim to ensure investment security for the preservation and further development of the company, while simultaneously taking advantage of the currently positive sentiment in the commodity markets.

Operating And Financial Situation As Of Q3-2025 Results

Operations

About the main commodity, copper, Kansanshi mine contributes 45%, Sentinel 49% and the other sites 6% to the total consolidated production.

  • First Quantum's results for the third quarter of 2025 show a 9.9% decrease in copper production year-over-year, but a 14.9% increase compared to the previous quarter. This increase is attributable to the expansion of Kansanshi S3, higher throughput at Sentinel, and 24,306 tonnes of copper from Cobre Panama under the P&SM plan. The results also show that the S3 project is on budget at US$1.25 billion, with 86.4% of costs allocated through the third quarter of 2025. Work continues on Ball Mill 2 and Underground Crusher 2 at Sentinel. Despite this progress, however, sustained annual growth has not yet been achieved. According to First Quantum's Q3-2025 results report, the company states that

Copper sales volumes totalled 118,825 tonnes, approximately 14,199 tonnes higher than production due to the shipment of 24,306 tonnes of copper from Cobre Panamá during the quarter with the approval of the Preservation and Safe Management (“P&SM”) plan by the Government of Panama (“GOP”).

In contrast to the negative year-on-year production trend, sales of the red metal rose by 6% and were 17.4% higher than the previous quarter.

  • AISC per pound of copper decreased 6.4% sequentially but increased 26.9% year-over-year, primarily due to improved production in Q3 2025 compared to Q2 2025 and lower copper production year-over-year.

Financials

EBITDA increased to $435 million, driven by higher copper sales and prices, although gains were partially limited by hedging that secured lower prices for most of its sales. The improvement is not yet sustainable year-on-year, with uncertainty surrounding future support from Cobre Panama, whose resumed deliveries helped reverse a negative EBITDA trend in Q3 2025. Operating cash flow increased to $1.195 billion and net debt decreased to $4.751 billion, supported by higher EBITDA and a $1 billion upfront, non-debt form payment from a gold streaming agreement with Royal Gold. The company also invested $280 million in mineral property improvements, paid $166 million in interest and added $136 million to working capital. In summary, we believe that it will not be an easy task to match Q3 2025 cash flow and EBITDA on an annual basis without continued support from Panama. But we remain confident that the upcoming relaxation of hedging contracts will allow an additional 20% of copper production and sales (up to 50% of the total) to be priced at open market copper prices in H1-2026 in an overall positive environment.

Outlook: Copper Production, Costs, Prices, And CapEx

In its Q3 2025 results report, First Quantum announced the following:

Copper production guidance has narrowed from 380,000 – 440,000 tonnes to 390,000 – 410,000 tonnes,

Gold production guidance has narrowed from 135,000 – 155,000 tonnes to 140,000 – 150,000 ounces,

Nickel production guidance has narrowed from 15,000 – 25,000 tonnes to 18,000 – 23,000 tonnes,

Mid-range copper production expectations appear to have been lowered by 10,000 tons. This could indicate a slowdown in copper production, with operating costs remaining more or less unchanged until the end of 2025. This could potentially impact equity sentiment until early 2026 when First Quantum will release Q4-2025 results. Also, readers may want to consider the effects of hedging contracts as well, which hinder full access to the highest prices on the commodity markets.

Copper Prices

In its Q3 2025 results report, First Quantum states:

As at October 28, 2025, the Company had zero cost copper collar contracts outstanding for 171,300 tonnes at weighted average prices of $4.12 per lb to $4.72 per lb with maturities to June 2026. Of these, there were 72,825 tonnes with maturities to the end of 2025 with weighted average prices of $4.11 per lb to $4.85 per lb.

We estimate that well over 70% of total production for the last two months of 2025 is hedged at weighted average prices of US$4.11 to US$4.85 per pound (with an average of US$4.48/pound, the range is 6 cents/pound more favourable than for the entire period until maturity in June 2026). However, as the company explains in its Q3 2025 results, this is because

The increase in hedged proportion of the remaining 2025 production is largely as a result of narrowed production guidance.

The company also states:

Approximately 70% of remaining planned production and sales in 2025, and approximately 50% of planned production and sales for the first half of 2026 are protected from spot copper price movements. […]. In addition, as at October 28, 2025, the Company had zero cost gold collar contracts outstanding for 63,768 ounces at weighted average prices of $2,954 per oz to $4,215 per oz with maturities to June 2026.

Therefore, significantly less than 30% of the remaining planned production and sales in 2025 will benefit from the 16% increase in the copper spot price to an average of US$5.17/lb in the fourth quarter of 2025. In other words, more than 70% of the planned production and sales will not benefit from a copper price increase of US$0.70/lb (US$5.17 minus US$4.48), where US$4.48 represents the midpoint of the hedging contract price range calculated above. In the first half of 2026, only 50% of the remaining planned production and sales will benefit from higher copper prices. At the time of this writing, copper futures are trading at $5.63 per pound, representing a 20.6% increase from the average of $4.67 per pound in the first half of 2025. This means that the unhedged share of production and sales affected by rising copper prices will increase from around 30% to 50% in the first half of 2026. This share benefits from copper price growth, which is currently forecast at approximately 21% annually. Analysts view the copper price development as very positive.

Copper prices are reaching new highs because as Seeking Alpha reports

due to supply issues at major mines and significant changes in global trade linked to U.S. tariffs under President Trump.

This increase is set to make it the best year for copper since the 2009 financial crisis.

Gold Hedges

In its Q3 2025 results report, First Quantum also states:

In addition, as at October 28, 2025, the Company had zero cost gold collar contracts outstanding for 63,768 ounces at weighted average prices of $2,954 per oz to $4,215 per oz with maturities to June 2026.

These hedging transactions, which preclude more than 70% of gold production and sales to a 100% exposure to rising gold prices, are truly a limitation to the profit opportunity because the Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR) $4,468.58/oz (+70.86% year-to-date) benefits from strong safe-haven demand, robust central bank purchases, and the hope for interest rate cuts in 2026. It is likely to continue rising in 2026 due to “ an underlying bullish trend.”, according to Rania Gule, senior market analyst at XS.com, as reported by Yahoo Finance.

Capital Expenditures

First Quantum Minerals Ltd.'s Market Value

First Quantum Minerals Ltd. shares, listed on the US OTC under the symbol “FQVLF”, are trading at $26.97 per share and have a market capitalisation of $22.34 billion at the time of this writing.

Technical Analysis

The current stock price is near the upper end of the 52-week range of $10.18 to $27.10 per share. The RSI of 76.43 indicates overbought territory. Therefore, a correction from this market level could be imminent, although the stock's price action, as signaled by the 20-day (20-MA), 50-MA, and 200-MA moving averages in both the short and long term, is clearly bullish.

Valuation

Several indicators suggest that FQVLF stock is currently overvalued, both compared to the sector median and compared to the average value of its own ratio over the last 5 years, as illustrated by the screenshot of Seeking Alpha's analyst estimates:

The Risk Section

  • The Sprott mining risk map shows that Zambia and Mauritania pose a high risk, Turkey a medium risk, Panama an extreme risk, and Finland and Western Australia a minimal risk for onshore mining and exploration.

  • The Altman Z-score is 1.25, against a safe range starting from 3.0. The net debt-to-EBITDA ratio is $6,254 million to $1,728 million, which corresponds to a value of 3.6x (a value below 2 is highly desired). The interest coverage ratio is 2x, which is at the minimum acceptable level. The financial situation is cause for concern, and there is a real risk of financial difficulties or insolvency.
  • The AISC/lb copper is US$3.20 or US$7,055/tonne, which results in US$3.39 billion, as we have multiplied the AISC/tonne by 480,100 tonnes of copper equivalent, based on current metal prices and the metal production forecast for 2025. Adding US$845 million in non-sustainable capital expenditures (“CapEx”), the total financing requirement over a 12-month period is US$4.235 billion, which is divided by 480,100 tonnes of copper equivalent, leading to US$8,815/tonne or US$4/lb. These estimates compare to a hedge contract for 50% of production at weighted average prices of $4.12 per pound to $4.72 per pound maturing in June 2026, and to current copper prices at $5.63 per pound as of today, with the bright outlook. The comparison helps to mitigate the previously seen financial risk. Also, the debt of US$7,225.0 million consisted of senior notes whose maturity was extended to 2029 following refinancing and restructuring. Also, at the end of the third quarter of 2025, the company had US$971.0 million in cash and cash equivalents and short-term investments, as well as US$1,722 million in working capital. Also, Investors should note that First Quantum raises the capital needed for expenditures or to refinance existing debt by issuing bonds. This capital can come from both lenders and risk-tolerant investors in the financial markets. Alternatively, the company can secure capital through long-term bank loans, which represents a de-risked financing scenario, especially since banks are currently hesitant to grant corporate loans until they are certain of the applicants' creditworthiness. On the other hand, the fact that First Quantum issues bonds to investors with above-average risk tolerance ultimately suggests that the company could well live up to its claim of being a worthwhile investment.
  • The average trading volume of FQVLF shares (“over three months”) of 262,813 indicates low liquidity on a daily basis (see Seeking Alpha under “ Trading Data”, as of today). One drawback is that investors who need to reduce their positions may not find sufficient demand due to the low daily trading volume. If they have to significantly lower their selling price to sell shares, this could jeopardise the success of the trade. On the other hand, the low daily trading volume also creates an environment in which upward pressure on the share price can increase significantly, even in response to minor but impactful macroeconomic and geopolitical shifts—to which gold and copper are highly sensitive.

Conclusion

We think that a “hold” rating for First Quantum Minerals Ltd. is currently most appropriate, as this producer of primarily copper, but also gold and nickel, is overvalued relative to what we believe short-term expectations could offer in terms of profitability and further growth for the stock. The hype surrounding AI data centers, electrification, electric vehicles, and the clean energy transition has undoubtedly driven copper prices higher, as copper is a key metal in these projects. First Quantum's share price has waxed like crazy this year, much to the delight of shareholders. However, the concern is that these valuations are now overly optimistic given what we consider to be quite uncertain short-term expectations. The company faces a tighter production forecast and hedging contracts that limit its ability to capitalize on the sharp rise in copper prices, as well as the uncertain situation surrounding copper sales in Panama.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: After Reading This Article You Will Reconsider Your Position In IGLD (Downgrade)
data.description:
As an income investor, I see a clear merit of integrating covered call ETF products in my portfolio. Even though there is a plethora of high-yielding instruments out there, when it comes to current income sustainability the investable universe is much smaller.

In my view, apart from the selected BDCs (BIZD), MLPs (AMLP), REITs (VNQ) and closed- end funds (PDO), there is nothing much that would be interesting for, say, durable 7%+ yield investment strategies. Of course, we can here and there find some one-offs such as tobacco (BTI) (albeit, the yields in the sector have gone down to ~5% level) and high-quality leveraged ETFs (PFFA), but most likely these securities will anyways exhibit rather strong correlation with the base selection, which consists of the mentioned BDCs, MLPs etc.

So, in this context, covered call ETFs can definitely come in handy, acting as both income enhancers and diversifiers.

The former aspect is quite straightforward as typically covered call ETFs offer monthly dividends that yield close to or above 10%. The second aspect is a bit more subtle and actually the key reason why I issued a bullish thesis on the FT Vest Gold Strategy Target Income ETF (BATS:IGLD) earlier in October, this year.

The beauty of covered call ETFs (apart from yield factor) is that we, as income investors, can access asset classes and specific exposures, which typically do not fit at all with income-oriented strategy. For example, by investing in IGLD we can get the following:

  • Attractive yield.
  • Partial participation in gold's (XAUUSD:CUR) upside move.
  • Downside protection exactly the same as for gold with a slight edge stemming from the collected option premiums.

The same applies to the NEOS NASDAQ-100 High Income ETF (QQQI), which is exposed to the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ) (also an exposure, which we probably will not find suitable for income portfolio), the UBS ETRACS Silver Shares Covered Call ETN (SLVO) for silver (SLV) etc.

Now, going back to gold and IGLD.

Even though I am bullish on gold and have enjoyed solid returns from my IGLD investment, I have decided to liquidate the stake and direct the proceeds into the NEOS Gold High Income ETF (BATS:IAUI), which is very similar to IGLD - but with slight nuances that just make me more comfortable as a buy-and-hold long-term investor (see my original thesis on IAUI here).

Let me flesh out the reasoning why I have liquidated my profitable position in IGLD.

Thesis review

At its core, IGLD is a typical covered call ETF with similar exposures that we can get from any NEOS product like QQQI and the NEOS S&P 500(R) High Income ETF (SPYI). Namely, there is a partial upside participation, decent income streams stemming from collected option premiums and almost fully open downside that is slightly better protected than for pure-play index due to the option premium component.

We can nicely see the similarity between IGLD and IAUI in the chart below:

IGLD and IAUI returns are almost identical and both have underperformed gold because of the capped upside that is associated with sold call option strike values.

For me, the fact that both of these covered call ETFs have underperformed gold is completely fine because the motivation of going long here has not been to capture alpha over the underlying index, but to secure high current income streams with some price appreciation and gold-driven downside protection. Without the integration of optionality, we as income investors do not really have alternative ways to capture both worlds: enticing income and gold risk and return factors.

Scenario in which IGLD and IAUI could likely outperform the index is if the gold starts to trade sideways, gradually falls or gradually increases (at a very slow pace).

So, the question is again - why exit IGLD?

There are two reasons.

First, IGLD is inherently a lower yield producing instrument than IAUI. In the chart below it is quite evident how a notable part of IGLD's total return has come from price appreciation rather than dividends, which constitute a much greater share of the return figure for IAUI.

The thing is that IGLD has established a strategy to consistently generate ~3.85% over the one-month U.S. Treasury. So, it tailors the option overlay process to what is necessary to generate that level of income, leaving the rest of the exposure fully open to gold. For example, currently, the option overwrite is 30% of the total exposure. This is why we can see 7.1% yield for IGLD, but close to 12% for IAUI (if we annualize its most recent monthly payment).

In the context of high-income investing strategies, I think that one would expect much greater yield than 7% from making a move into covered call ETF space.

Second, and this is a bit more complex and, perhaps even a subjective reason. As opposed to most covered call ETFs, IGLD is a synthetically structured one, which means that it does not own the underlying gold at all. Instead, it carries a decent base of short-term U.S. Treasuries and does the following:

  • goes short on put options of GLD.
  • goes long on call options of GLD, thereby replicating a standard long GLD exposure.
  • goes short on a certain amount of at-the-money GLD call options to establish a covered call ETF exposure.

If we look back at how IGLD has performed since its inception, we will not see any risky or worrisiem deviations from the typical covered call ETF performance, which would be underpinned by physical gold holdings.

And most probably going forward there should not be any issues with that, which would render IGLD inferior to IAUI or other covered call ETF gold products, which actually own the underlying instead of having it as a synthetical exposure.

However, personally, I do not feel that comfortable owning a synthetically structured product in my portfolio. There is always a risk that stems from option rolling to maintain the gold exposure (e.g., wide option bid-ask spreads that could come from an extremely volatile market, clearing house risk). In other words, the systematic risk is higher for a synthetical covered call ETF than a physical vehicle.

The bottom line

In my view, it is definitely worth keeping a gold-linked covered call ETF product in income portfolios in order to add in the diversification from the Nasdaq-100 and typical value-biased yield instruments. Plus, gold might do very well in case we see damaged Fed's credibility, further depreciation of dollar or even more intensified geopolitical situation.

However, due to the relatively unattractive dividend yield and, importantly, the lack of physical gold holding, I have decided to exit IGLD, putting that capital into alternative gold-based covered call ETFs (e.g., IAUI).

data.title: Riot Platforms: Stock Could Drop To The Single Digits In 2026
data.description:
Riot Platforms (RIOT) is facing some serious fundamental and technical risks for 2026. My theory is that Bitcoin (BTC-USD) is repeating its crypto winter or bear market, which occurs every four years. The last two price cycles were consistent, lasting exactly 1,050 days from the price bottom to the price peak. My theory is that this price cycle already peaked in October 2025, which would have marked the same length of time from the price bottom from the end of 2022. Bitcoin's bear markets tend to last about one year from the peak price.

Since Riot's stock price action is highly correlated to bitcoin's, the stock is likely to experience a significant drop. Riot's stock tends to decline over 95% during the bitcoin bear markets. So, it is not worth owning Riot during the bear markets. I don't know if Riot will drop that drastically this time, but the stock does tend to decline more than bitcoin percentage-wise during the crypto winters.

Bearish Technical Set-up

Bitcoin's monthly chart supports my thesis that we are already in the crypto winter for this price cycle. Bitcoin's bear markets have been confirmed when the MACD makes a bearish crossover. This occurs when the blue MACD line crosses below the red signal line and the histogram bars turn red. The bearish crossover occurred in November as Bitcoin made a significant drop from over $100k down to about $80k before recovering.

What also stands out about the price cycles is that bearish divergences formed at the peaks. A bearish divergence occurred from April 2021 to November 2021 as bitcoin's price made a higher high while the RSI made a lower high. The same thing occurred from December 2024 to October 2025. Bearish divergences like this typically result in significant reversals from what I have observed.

I provided Riot's weekly chart to display the head & shoulders pattern that formed from July 2025 to November 2025. July formed the left shoulder, while November formed the right shoulder. The head of the pattern formed in October. Head & shoulder patterns tend to result in significant drops to key support levels in my observations. These support levels are in the single digits for Riot's stock. That is where I think the stock is headed in 2026.

Riot's weekly chart is already bearish as the RSI moved below the 50 level. The MACD is also bearish, as the blue line crossed below the red signal line while the histogram bars turned red. I expect the stock to test the $10 to $12 area before dropping further to the $6 area in 2026. If the price breaks below $6, the stock could drop to the low single digits. Riot's stock did drop to the $3 to $4 range in the last price cycle. Of course, this time, Riot may have peaked at a much lower price than previous price cycles, so we could see a very low price at the bottom of this bear market.

Riot's Fundamental Issues

While I believe that bitcoin's price action will have the biggest impact on Riot's stock in 2026, the company also has some fundamental issues that could derail the stock. The main fundamental issue for Riot has been the struggle to maintain and grow profitability.

I do acknowledge that Riot benefitted from Bitcoin's higher prices in 2025. The company's cost to mine bitcoin, excluding depreciation, was $46,324 in Q3 2025. However, the cost to mine when bitcoin miner depreciation is included was about $89,000. Riot is at risk of significant net losses as the price of Bitcoin dropped to $87,000. My opinion is that the price of Bitcoin will continue to decline in 2026 as part of its cycle's bear market or crypto winter.

The price of Bitcoin has historically dropped about 80% during the year-long bear markets. A similar drop this cycle could take the price down to $25,200 from the high of $126,000 in this bear market. It may not drop that far, but the possibility is there.

If the price of Bitcoin does decline in 2026 and remains lower than Riot's $89k cost to mine, it would likely cause Riot to experience a net loss for the year. Margins are likely to be significantly narrowed if this occurs. Analysts estimate that Riot will have an EPS loss of -$0.52 for 2026. This would be an over 4x higher loss than the expected EPS loss of -$0.12 for 2025. Such a large increase in net losses is likely to drive the stock lower in 2026.

Riot has been developing its Corsicana facility over the past few years. When Corsicana is completed, it is expected to generate 1 gigawatt (GW) of bitcoin mining and data center hosting power. However, Riot currently only has 400 megawatts online for bitcoin mining operations. This leaves 600 megawatts left to be built out and utilized. Riot plans on using the remaining capacity for critical IT infrastructure. This is likely to be used for AI and high-performance computing applications.

It may take a few years to get the remaining 600 megawatts up and running. This means that the cash flow from the 400 megawatts may not compensate for the unused portion of Corsicana in the short term, especially in 2026. Construction of the two Core and Shell buildings for the data center portion of Corsicana is expected to begin in Q1 2026 and is not expected to be completed until 2027. Riot will be incurring CapEx of $214 million within the next 18 months for these 2 buildings.

While I think that Corsicana can be a positive driver of long-term growth for Riot, I think it will hurt margins in 2026 due to the high costs associated with it. The high costs may put negative pressure on the stock in 2026.

Valuation

Since Riot Platforms is not yet sustainably profitable, I used the price/sales ratio for the valuation. Riot is trading with a forward price/sales of 7.8x. This is more than 2x higher than the sector median price/sales of 3.4x.

Riot is expected to earn $757 million in revenue in 2026, according to consensus estimates. This would be 14% higher than the $663 million in revenue that analysts expected Riot to earn in 2025. That's not bad revenue growth. However, if Bitcoin's price continues to decline in 2026 as part of a bear market, the revenue estimate could be lowered. Plus, the expected net loss could be worse than expected.

Since Riot is trading at a premium valuation as compared to the sector median, I think the stock is poised to drop further. The valuation is likely to be reset as it typically does during bitcoin bear markets.

We can see in the chart above that Riot's forward price/sales ratio dropped to about two at the price bottom of the last bitcoin bear market back in early 2023. It looks like the forward price/sales is trending further down as the bitcoin bear market continues. It is likely that it will continue to drop to the low single digits in 2026 if this bear market is similar to the previous one.

Risks to the Thesis

My thesis that Bitcoin will have a bear market that is similar to the previous price cycles might turn out to be different this time. Some analysts believe that this time is different because more institutional investments into Bitcoin will lead to an avoidance of the bear markets of the past. Higher demand from large investors could provide better support for bitcoin's price. However, people were saying something similar back in 2021, but a year-long bear market occurred anyway.

It is also possible that Riot performs better than I expect if the price of Bitcoin remains above the $89k cost to mine for most of 2026. Riot could beat analysts' expectations, driving the stock higher.

The Bottom Line

If you believe that Bitcoin and Riot will experience a year-long bear market that is similar to previous price cycles, selling your shares will probably be a wise decision. Riot's stock price tends to drop significantly during the bitcoin bear markets. Bitcoin and Riot may bottom approximately in September 2026 if this price cycle is similar to previous cycles. At that point, we can evaluate if buying back in would be a good idea.

data.title: Huntington Ingalls: Sailing The High Seas
data.description:
Recap

I covered Huntington Ingalls Industries, Inc. (HII) a little more than a year ago following the release of their Q3 2024 earnings. At that time the stock traded for about $188 per share and appeared about 13% overvalued based on my custom FCF valuation model. Fast forward twelve months, and the stock has been on an incredible run, earning a total return of close to 95%, while the S&P 500 (SP500), as measured by (SPY), is up a little more than 15%.

With all that price movement taking place, let's look at Huntington Ingalls again and see what's changed. Is it still a high-quality company? Is the dividend still safe and growing? And lastly, what did the most recent earnings call say, and where might the stock go from here?

Quality Metrics

With any company I consider investing in, there are three important quantitative measurements I always look at to determine if it is a high-quality business. Those characteristics are a growing revenue stream, reliable profit margins, and a healthy return on invested capital.

The revenue for HII does fluctuate some due to the nature of their business, with minimal growth in 2024 of around 2%. However, the 5-year CAGR is 5.8%, while the 10-year CAGR is a more impressive 7.6%. Although revenue growth is never going to be anything spectacular, I expect it to continue to climb as the United States' aging fleet is decommissioned and replaced.

The gross profit margin is typically in the mid-to-upper teens, with some years better than others. Over the past twelve months, though, this metric has surged to more than 27.6%, the highest over the past decade, and the first time it has eclipsed 20% since 2018.

The return on invested capital hasn't changed much over the last twelve months and is still hovering around 5%. Unfortunately, this metric has struggled post-pandemic, and for the time being, I don't expect this to change much, barring more efficient use of the company's capital.

Dividend Data

Currently, HII pays a quarterly dividend of $1.38 per share, or $5.52 annually, giving it a forward dividend yield of 1.55%. Earlier this quarter, the company raised its dividend a mere 2.2%, well below its 3-, 5-, and 10-year dividend growth rates, which are 4.3%, 5.1%, and 12.3%, respectively. Although the increases have been subpar in recent years, the company still has ample room for growth, with the payout ratio not surpassing 40% at any point during the company's history.

Earnings Update

In late October, Huntington Ingalls announced their Q3 2025 earnings with a double beat. GAAP earnings per share were $3.68, topping the consensus by $0.32, on revenue of $3.2B, which exceeded analysts expectations by around $250M and was an increase of 18.5% YoY.

Looking at the Ingalls shipyard first, revenue surged nearly 25% year-over-year to $828M, from $664M, while operating income grew by $16M to $65M for the quarter; both of these results were due to strong demand for surface combatants. Additionally, the operating margin expanded by 50 basis points to almost 8%, for the same reason as the revenue and operating income growth.

The Newport News segment earned strong revenue growth too, rising more than $200M compared to Q3 2024 and coming in at $1.6B for the quarter, an increase of 15%. Operating income for Newport News grew exponentially to $80M, an increase of more than 400%. This segment also saw strong margin expansion, with the operating margin expanding nearly 400 bps to 5% for the quarter.

Finally, the Mission Technologies portion of the business also earned double-digit revenue growth, increasing 11% compared to the same quarter a year ago. Revenue for MT was $787M for this quarter, while operating income was essentially flat YoY, and the operating margin contracted minimally. The main drivers behind these results were strong demand in cyber, electronic warfare, and live and virtual training modules.

Immediately following this earnings call, shares of the stock rose almost 7%, and overall since this earnings release, HII has risen close to 20%.

In more recent news, earlier this week President Trump announced plans to create a new "Trump Class" battleship in an effort to revitalize American shipbuilding and help return the U.S. Navy to dominance. It's well known the United States has an aging fleet of military ships, with an approximate average age of close to 25 years. Initially, it appears the Navy will start with two battleships, but it's possible the total number could reach 25 when it is all said and done.

Overall, I believe there are pros and cons to this decision, with the obvious being the U.S. Navy getting a fleet modernization, but also this area of the market getting an industrial boost with the implementation of cutting-edge technologies along with the creation of many jobs. Also, as simple as it sounds, updating the Navy's fleet could be a strong deterrence, as heavily armed ships could provide multiple options for the Navy, such as missile defense, coupled with a powerful look.

Conversely, with a potential total of 25 ships, this could likely lead to a massive spending increase in the military budget, which may be tough to sell to Congress, along with possibly diverting funds away from other more pressing needs such as submarines, drones, and cyber defenses. Moreover, although this would lead to a complete modernization of the Navy, it's possible this type of ship is outdated, as many of today's conflicts focus on stealth and unmanned systems as opposed to giant ships.

Valuation

The final consideration is to determine if HII is trading for an attractive valuation at its current levels, and as per usual, we'll examine it from a free cash flow perspective.

I estimate a stock's implied fair value based on its free cash flow yield and specifically how the current yield compares to its historical average. This helps me gauge how much investors have typically been willing to pay for a company's free cash flow and how attractive the stock looks today relative to that history. The analysis is calculated on 12 years of daily historical price data, capturing the rolling 5-year average free cash flow yield for the past 7 years.

The key assumption behind this valuation model is that investors have paid a fair price for the stock over the past 5 years, on average. However, since every valuation is an estimate, there is a +/-10% margin of error applied to the fair value range.

My custom FCF valuation model indicates the stock is trading for a small discount to its implied fair value, with a current price of about $355 and a fair price closer to $375.

The long-term expected rate of return is more than double what it was a year ago, with the biggest change coming from the expected earnings growth rate increasing from 7.36% to more than 13.5%. Also, the forward dividend yield is now 1.55%, and the return to fair value factor contributes another 0.98%.

Final Thoughts

In summary, HII has seen incredible price appreciation over the last twelve months, and despite the rise in stock price, it still appears to have a little more room to run. The financials and dividend are never going to blow investors out of the water, but they are predictable, and that's a good thing for the market. The most recent earnings report showed the company clicking on all cylinders with double-digit growth across all segments of the business. Overall, HII has earned itself a BUY rating at this time with solid growth across the company as well as a favorable valuation.

data.title: SCHD's Dividend Growth Formula Is Broken (For Now)
data.description:
Written by Austin Rogers for High Yield Investor.

The highly popular Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF™ (SCHD) has earned a reputation as the best of the best among dividend ETFs.

Historically, it has done the seemingly impossible, allowing investors to have their cake and eat it too by producing double-digit dividend growth while offering an above-average yield typically between 2.75% and 3.75%.

Why choose between current income and strong dividend growth when you can have it all with SCHD?

During the first decade of SCHD's existence as an ETF, it outperformed both of Vanguard's popular dividend-focused ETFs: the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG) and the Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM):

And, remarkably, SCHD outperformed these two much larger, more established, and lower turnover funds while generating significantly stronger dividend growth:

Surely any dividend ETF capable of accomplishing such a feat over the span of a full decade is very special. We discussed this at great length in a recent article comparing SCHD to covered call ETFs.

But despite liking SCHD a lot, it is fair to note its recent performance has been rather disappointing, and this is going to be the focus of today's follow-up.

We won't sugarcoat it. We believe SCHD's unique formula for outperformance and double-digit dividend growth is broken -- at least for now.

Below, we explain what went wrong over the last few years and some thoughts about what to do about it.

Recent Reconstitutions Are Killing SCHD's Performance

There are two main mechanisms of fundamental return performance for an ETF like SCHD:

  1. Portfolio holdings' earnings/dividend growth.
  2. Algorithmic portfolio recycling from appreciated low-yielders to underpriced high-yielders.

The ETF's basket of individual stock holdings could collectively enjoy earnings and dividend growth, which increases their fundamental value and, all else being equal, should cause stock price appreciation.

But on top of that, the ETF is reconstituting its 100-stock portfolio annually and rebalancing that portfolio quarterly. By design, its stock-picking algorithm sells its appreciated, lower-yielding holdings in order to reinvest in higher-yielding stocks.

During a broad-based bull market, there are a wide array of rapidly rising stocks as well as some stocks in temporary dips. This gives SCHD's algorithm the opportunity to regularly realize gains from appreciated names and reinvest into higher-yielding names.

Here's SCHD's price performance compared to fellow 3%+ yielding dividend ETFs like the First Trust Morningstar Dividend Leaders ETF (FDL), WisdomTree US High Dividend ETF (DHS), and iShares Core High Dividend ETF (HDV) from around 2012 through Q1 2024:

Around half of SCHD's extraordinary, double-digit annual dividend growth rate is attributable to this portfolio recycling algorithm. When it works, it works really well.

One example is the broad-based bull market during the decade from 2013 through 2023. In this period, portfolio recycling juiced a compounding degree of total dividend growth compared to low-turnover dividend ETFs like the Vanguard High Dividend ETF:

While VYM's dividend growth relies overwhelmingly on the collective dividend growth of its underlying holdings, SCHD enjoys the two engines of dividend growth mentioned above: (1) Organic growth from its basket of holdings and (2) accretive portfolio recycling.

But in recent years, especially since the release of ChatGPT in late 2022, the performance of most non-AI-related stocks has been mediocre to poor.

We no longer have a broad-based bull market. We have an extremely top-heavy one.

The AI-tech ecosystem is generating robust earnings growth, although most of it originates within that ecosystem rather than from paying customers outside of it. Meanwhile, large parts of the U.S. economy outside the AI-tech space are languishing from a frozen housing market, virtually zero net job growth, falling wage growth, and a financially fragile paycheck-to-paycheck class.

As such, SCHD's algorithmic portfolio recycling engine has been shut off.

Worse, the last two reconstitutions in March 2024 and March 2025 have actually put the ETF in an even more disadvantageous position amid the current environment.

The last two reconstitutions have systematically purged its portfolio of many stocks still capable of material growth and replaced them with stocks burdened by poor fundamentals and headwinds.

We'll demonstrate this to you.

In the chart below, we compare the total return performance of SCHD and several 3%+ yielding dividend ETF peers, including:

  • SPDR S&P 500 High Dividend ETF (SPYD)
  • TBG Dividend Focus ETF (TBG)
  • VictoryShares US Lg Cap High Dividend Vol-Weighted ETF (CDL)
  • First Trust Morningstar Dividend Leaders ETF.

As you can see, SCHD was performing more or less in line with its high-yielding dividend ETF peers from late 2023 through Q1 2024.

But at SCHD's March 2024 reconstitution (red line on the left), the ETF sold its nearly 5%-weighted position in Broadcom (AVGO). Since then, AVGO has almost tripled in price and been one of the winners of the AI revolution. The proceeds from selling AVGO and other winners were reinvested mostly into names that have been fairly rangebound ever since -- such as Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) and Hershey (HSY).

This caused SCHD to begin underperforming peers like TBG that continued to hold AVGO for a while, as well as SPYD and CDL that didn't double-down on poorly performing stocks.

On the positive side, the 2024 reconstitution also caused a nice, if one-time, jump in SCHD's dividend payout.

And then in the March 2025 reconstitution (red line on the right above), the same vicious cycle played out again as the ETF jettisoned names like BlackRock (BLK) that have performed quite well since the recon, while adding a plethora of names that have underperformed since the recon.

Merck (MRK) and Target (TGT) are two such underperformers added this past March, but the biggest additions came from the upstream energy sector, including ConocoPhillips (COP) and Schlumberger (SLB). This suddenly gave energy the largest sectoral weighting in SCHD's portfolio at over 20%.

Immediately after the late-March reconstitution, President Trump's "Liberation Day" tariff announcement crushed the energy sector, which has not recovered since.

Hence SCHD's continued underperformance against peers since late March:

It is not necessarily that SCHD's portfolio is any weaker than that of its peers.

It's that its last two annual reconstitutions have put it further and further behind the curve, disposing of strong performers and acquiring weaker performers.

And by "perform," here, we are mostly referring to price performance. The quality of margins and balance sheets of SCHD's portfolio has not declined with each reconstitution. Rather, the ETF has disproportionately shed high-quality stocks that are in the midst of uptrends while disproportionately adding high-quality stocks that lack fundamental catalysts and/or are in cyclical downturns.

Another way to illustrate this is by comparing the performance of SCHD to the sector ETFs of its two largest sectoral weightings -- energy (XLE) and consumer staples (XLP) -- since its latest reconstitution:

Given that energy and staples make up about 38% of SCHD's portfolio, it's unsurprising to find that SCHD's price performance has been right in the middle of these two sectoral indices.

Our Takeaways

What are our takeaways from the preceding analysis?

Here are some thoughts:

  1. One of SCHD's two engines of dividend growth (arguably its most important one) is currently out of commission. With few holdings that have highly appreciated over the last few years, accretive portfolio recycling cannot happen.
  2. The broad-based bull market from SCHD's inception in 2011 to the end of 2021 provided the ideal environment for the ETF's portfolio recycling algorithm to work, and it did work swimmingly during this period.
  3. The increasingly top-heavy and AI-focused bull market over the last several years has been a uniquely bad environment for SCHD's algorithm. The virtuous cycle of 2011-2021 has become a vicious cycle from 2023 to the present.
  4. It would require another broad-based bull market -- or at least one that includes the dividend-heavy sectors like energy, staples, and financials -- to rejuvenate SCHD's portfolio recycling engine.
  5. Until a sufficient amount of internal strength within SCHD's portfolio shows up, the ETF's dividend growth will likely be cut in half and approximate that of its low-turnover dividend ETF peers.

Does any of this mean that SCHD suddenly holds nothing but junk? No. Its portfolio remains high-quality, albeit out of favor.

Does it mean that SCHD's unique formula is permanently broken? No, most likely not.

But does it mean that SCHD's portfolio construction methodology results in particularly poor performance during periods of tech-focused euphoria? Yes, it seems so.

We think SCHD's emphasis on quality, high returns on equity, significant cash flows, relatively low debt, and dividend growth make it a worthy anchor for a dividend portfolio. But that doesn't mean the ETF works equally well in all market environments.

The current AI-obsessed market turns out to be a uniquely bad one for SCHD, and its last two reconstitutions have only exacerbated its disadvantages in this environment.

Conclusion

In our view, long-term holders of SCHD have no pressing need to sell the ETF. On the contrary, now could be a great time to invest in it. It is undervalued at the moment, and there will likely come another broad-based bull market in the future in which SCHD excels again.

It would be reasonable to simply continue holding SCHD and collecting the dividends (which have not declined, even though their growth has temporarily stalled). But it might also be reasonable to trim a portion of one's SCHD position in order to reinvest in dividend ETFs with different strategies and portfolio construction methodologies.

The best path will probably depend on an individual investor's outlook on the market and specifically the energy sector, which makes up SCHD's largest sectoral weighting.

At High Yield Investor, we have learned a lot from observing and studying SCHD's success over the years, including both the best and the worst of its reconstitution moves.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 251: $25,100 Allocated, $2,668.70 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
The economic news continues to paint a bullish scenario for the economy and the market. The latest CPI report came in at 2.7%, which was its lowest number since July and well below the forecast of 3.1%. What may be a larger shock to some is that the annual core CPI report, which excludes food and energy, fell to 2.6% in November, which was under the 3% estimate. Some of the main contributors are that crude is down -16.11% YoY as its under $60 per barrel, while Brent is down -14.53% YoY to around $62 per barrel. As commodity prices fall, the price of inputs declines, which is felt throughout the economy. The S&P 500 continues to make new highs, and getting to 7,000 by the end of the year isn’t as crazy as it once seemed. Inflation is down, interest rates are coming down, unemployment is low, and GDP expansion continues to heat up. This is a market that has legs, and I believe the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is structured well for the future, as many of the holdings can benefit from a lower rate environment. I’m excited to continue allocating capital toward the portfolio as I still believe there is a lot of value left to be unlocked throughout the portfolio.

The market continues to inch its way higher, and we’re getting closer to seeing a 7 handle on the S&P 500. After allocating $25,100 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 251 at $33,096.76. The account's profitability increased (1.98%) to $7,996.76, which is a return on invested capital of 31.86%. This week, I added to my positions in Verizon (VZ) and Starwood Property Trust (STWD). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $47.02 of dividend income in week 251. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $13.29 (0.50%) to $2,668.70. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $5,492.69 of income since its inception, which is 22.06% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. Heading into the end of the year, I believe that financials are going to lead the way higher as we see more loan origination volume due to a lower rate environment, while capital continues to flow into technology as many still believe we are in the early innings of an AI revolution. I am excited to continue adding to my positions and building out the income stream.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

I started this account with $100 with the goal of documenting how I would build out an income-producing portfolio from the ground up on a budget of $100 per week. The reality is that this type of investing isn’t for everyone, as there is a large cohort of investors looking to generate as much alpha as possible. While I have many investments for generating capital appreciation, I also have many investments in income producing assets. I am planning on continuing this account for another 20 years in addition to my other income investments, with a goal of generating enough income to live off without touching my actual retirement assets. I am now generating $2,668.70 in forward dividend income while being up $7,996.76 on invested capital. This account continues to impress me, and it's serving as a blueprint for what is possible with just $100 per week. Over time, this account will be generating 5-figures of income, and I am excited to see where the portfolio finishes in 2025 and how 2026 unfolds.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $877.35 (32.88%)
  • Equities $623.49 (23.36%)
  • REITs $452.19 (16.94%)
  • CEFs $447.52 (16.77%)
  • BDCs $268.14 (10.05%)

Investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off of them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself for 30 years in the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year, as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 51 weeks in 2025, $2,313.01 of dividend income was produced, which is 142.50% of the total income from 2024. There have been 787 dividends produced in 2025, which is 111% of the dividends generated in 2024.

There was $47.02 of dividend income generated this week, which brought the average amount of dividend income I have generated on a weekly basis to $45.35. This is an increase of $14.14 (45.30%) YoY, and there have been four weeks that exceeded $100, unlike in 2024, when the largest week was $89.13. I'm really excited for the rest of October to unfold, as the YoY data is showing me a huge trend going up and to the right with dividend income. Over the next several years, I should be able to exceed $5,000 in forward dividend income in this account and basically double my annualized allocation to the portfolio.

This week there was 447.02 of dividend income generated, which took Decembers income up to $138.41. There is still several trading days left in December, and I still believe that December will end up being a strong month for income as many CEFs and ETFs will be distributing long and short-term capital gains. When I look at the table below, I am getting very excited for 2026, as there is a real chance that every month except for February could exceed $200 of dividend income.

There are currently 41 positions accomplishing this, which is emphasized by the green section of the table below. There's $288.37 of forward dividend income being generated from the new dividends being generated from all the positions generating at least one share from their dividends annually. Many positions are still lagging, but I'm making a strong effort to allocate capital toward them so I can get at least 50% of the portfolio to accomplish this goal by the end of the year. My fixation on this is because it will allow me to advance the compounding impacts from the dividend snowball, and it also makes sure that I am not investing in the same positions each week.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

ETFs are exceeding 20% of the portfolio again, and it's entirely my fault. I was also adding REITs heading into the rate cut, which caused the portfolio to get a bit top-heavy. It's difficult for me to stay away from these sectors, especially after big tech crushed earnings. I'm going to work on evening out the sectors and having them more proportional in the future. Individual equities now represent 40.11% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio while generating 23.36% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 59.89% of the portfolio and generate 76.64% of the forward income. I'm working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have sectors close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5-10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

Altria Group (MO) is still tittering around the 4% level, which I will take considering it was pushing against my limit of 5% for far too long. The top 20 largest holdings have continued to even out throughout 2025, and I am excited about the fact that the portfolio is getting less top heavy. I have tried to refrain from adding too many of these positions excessively, even though I feel there is a lot of value to be unlocked. I am happy that this has turned out to be a diversified portfolio that can protect me from fluctuations in the market. I will continue to work on building a well-rounded portfolio, so I am protected from investments that don’t perform how I believe they would.

The top 10 holdings didn’t change this week, only the allocations as I added to VZ and STWF. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,737.20, and they finished the week with a value of $9,404.31. This is an ROI of $2,667.11 (39.59%). There has been $1,311.24 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 19.46% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $668.71 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 9.93%. These positions represent 28.41% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 25.06% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 251 Additions

This week I added to Verizon (VZ) and Starwood Property Trust (STWD).

Verizon

  • VZ is an interesting company, as many investors are obsessed with the $126.96 billion in long-term debt on the balance sheet, but seem to overlook the fact that they generate $50.19 billion in EBITDA on an annualized basis
  • VZ has more than enough profitability to cover the interest expenses, maintain the dividend, and pay down debt
  • VZ's new CEO, Dan Schulman, has a tremendous amount of turnaround experience and is incentivized with performance-based equity tied to higher share prices
  • Management is incentivized to unlock value for shareholders
  • VZ under $40 with close to a 7% yield is a huge deal in my opinion

Starwood Property Trust

  • STWD is trading around the $18.50 level, and its dividend yield is exceeding 10% again
  • I believe that STWD is offering a stable, high-yield stream of income, as the quarterly payments have never been missed throughout different economic cycles
  • STWD’s diversified capital deployment, including $10.2B invested YTD and a major net lease acquisition, positions it for earnings growth as rates decline
  • Management anticipates 2026 will be strong for REITs, with lower rates likely to boost book value, transaction activity, and capital appreciation potential
  • STWD is one of my favorite REITs, and I will continue to add to it when opportunities present themselves, such as these

Week 252 Gameplan

Next week I am planning on adding to my positions in the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) and the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq 100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ)

Conclusion

There is only a few trading days left in 2025, and the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is navigating the markets well. There is $2,668.70 of dividend income being generated on an annualized basis, which works out to $222.39 in monthly income. I am considering doing something that I haven’t done before and conducting a RIF by eliminating positions that represent less than 1% of the portfolio. I may look for a dollar amount instead, but I think that it’s time to conduct a portfolio clean up heading into 2026. Overall, things are looking good heading into the new year, and I believe that this portfolio is going to stand the test of time and expand the amount of income it's generating by a large amount, as all of the future income is reinvested. Please let me know what you think about the portfolio clean up. I may be eliminating 20 positions or so and reallocating that capital into high conviction names. Please leave all of your suggestions below in the comment section.

data.title: Agnico Eagle Mines: One Of My Top Picks For 2026
data.description:
Thesis

I think the gold run still has more legs in 2026. I think Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM) (AEM:CA) is a good way to play this theme:

  1. An accommodative Fed policy stance is bullish for gold
  2. Robust central bank buying is supportive for gold prices
  3. We are still early in the global gold ETF stocking cycle
  4. AEM is poised to see a modest production growth acceleration
  5. AEM is one of the global low-cost producers of gold
  6. AEM seems attractively valued on a relative basis
  7. The strong uptrend is likely to continue after a mini-hold

Note: $ in the article refers to USD, unless specified otherwise.

An accommodative Fed policy stance is bullish for gold

As gold (XAUUSD:CUR) is a non-yielding asset/instrument, it does better in dovish rate environments. And this is what we are heading into in 2026. Looking one year out, we see that there is a 92.9% chance of a lower Fed Funds rate than the current 350-375 bps:

Gold rallies are often supported when the Fed shifts to adding liquidity as well. And it looks like this is also happening as quantitative tightening has been halted:

Another key factor is the Fed’s shift in balance-sheet strategy. Its decision to halt Quantitative Tightening (QT) marks an inflection point from balance sheet contraction toward a more supportive stance. Recent use of liquidity facilities, including a roughly $50B draw on repo backstops, shows the Fed is willing to inject reserves when funding stress appears.

- State Street Investment Management

Robust central bank buying is supportive for gold prices

Globally, central banks have increased the metric ton volume of net gold purchases over the last few years. 2026 is expected to see a continuation of the same trend:

This constructive outlook is supported by the Central Bank Gold Reserves Survey 2025, where an increasing portion of central banks (71 surveyed in total in 2025) are saying they expect a moderately higher contribution of gold reserves 5 years out:

We are still early in the global gold ETF stocking cycle

The supporting macro environment for gold is driving strong gold ETF demand and thus another gold re-stocking cycle. This is when physically backed gold ETFs move from being net sellers of gold (de‑stocking) to sustained net buyers. The chart below shows how compared to previous cycles, we are still early into the current re-stocking cycle, likely with 2-3 more years of a positive demand environment to go:

AEM is poised to see a modest production growth acceleration

AEM is ramping up its capex investments over the past year as its spend has increased from around $400 million per quarter to the $600 million range as of Q3 FY25:

This capex increase seems rather sustainable and funded via increased cash flow from operations [CFO] as capex as a % of CFO has not increased materially:

Looking at the mix of capex spends, it is clear that the company is ramping up on more development/growth capex:

There are 5 major capex development projects in Canada with Detour Lake, Canadian Malartic (Odyssey), Upper Beaver, Hope Bay, as well as in Mexico in San Nicolas. Over the next 5-8 years, these projects are expecting to lead to a 20% increase in production:

On an aggregate basis, these projects can add close to 1.3 million to 1.5 million ounces of annual production in the next 5 to 8 years. This won't be fully additive. There will be some production coming off, especially if you think about our Meadowbank operation in Nunavut and Pinos Altos operation in Mexico, which are close to end of life. However, unlike with these 5 projects, we see the potential to grow production by about 20% over the next 5 to 8 years.

- VP of Investor Relations Jean-Marie Clouet in the Emerging Growth Conference 2025

This implies a gold production growth uptick from the 2.0% past 3-yr CAGR to a 2.8% CAGR outlook:

AEM is one of the global low-cost producers of gold

Over the last year, AEM has averaged all-in sustaining costs [AISC] of close to $1300/oz:

In the global gold AISC curve, this puts AEM in a rather favorable position of being in the second decile, making the company one of the low cost producers of gold:

As the company does not hedge against gold prices, AEM offers investors direct exposure to benefit from rising gold prices, which leads to improved economics via higher adjusted EBITDAs per ounce of gold produced:

AEM seems attractively valued on a relative basis

Compared to its other precious mining peers, AEM is trading at a 1-yr fwd P/CFPS (cash flow per share) multiple of 11.1x, which is exactly equal to the comps' median level:

But AEM has typically commanded a premium of 31.2% vs. its peers. One reason for the typical premium valuation is the fact that more than 97% of the company's global gold production is in countries such as Canada, Finland and Australia, which have much lower jurisdictional risks. Hence, the current 0% premium represents close to trough relative valuations over the past year, making AEM stock look attractive:

The strong uptrend is likely to continue after a mini-hold

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of AEM vs SPX500

On the relative monthly charts of AEM vs. SPX500, we see an incumbent strong and healthy uptrend. Over the last 3 months, the ratio prices have consolidated in a tight hold. But now in Dec'25, it looks like a bullish hammer candle is in-the-making, signaling an uptrend resumption.

Takeaway & Positioning

I am bullish on gold in 2026 due to an accommodative Fed policy outlook and increased buying from central banks. These factors are fueling higher flows into gold ETFs, sparking a new stocking cycle for which we are still in the early stages.

I like the gold miner Agnico Eagle Mines as a way to play the bullish gold theme as it offers direct, unhedged protection to gold prices, has advantageous economics via a second-decile position in the global gold-producing cost curve, and is poised for production growth acceleration in the medium to longer term. The valuations are attractive as well as AEM, despite its safer operational exposure to safer jurisdictions is not trading at its usual premium vs. other gold-mining peers. The technicals also point bullish, suggesting a continuation of the overall uptrend.

Obviously, the main risk to this thesis is a correction or crash in the gold prices. To monitor for this, we should keep an ongoing tab on the very factors that lead to a constructive view; rates and quantitative tightening policies, central banks' gold demand and gold ETF flows.

Rating: 'Strong Outperform/Strong Buy'

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Outperform/Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Outperform/Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Market Perform/Hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Underperform/Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Strong Underperform/Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.

data.title: The $100,000 Dividend Plan: Why Cash Flow Is The Only Retirement Goal That Matters
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

Today, I want to have a "back to the basics" article discussing an important topic for the upcoming year. The New Year is a time for us to look back at what we did right or wrong and look forward to what we want to achieve in the future.

Setting goals is an important component in any plan, so that you know what you are trying to achieve, you can track whether you are on pace to achieve it, and you know if what you're doing is working.

When it comes to investing, the most common goal that people are trying to achieve is to be able to fund their retirement. Also, something that many people struggle with is coming up with a goal that is both specific and realistic.

The Importance of Being Specific

"I want to have billions" – ok, that's nice, but are you going to make billions from having $500,000 in your portfolio and working a salary job that pays you $70,000/year? Probably not. Most people who have billions made millions by doing something extraordinary in their careers. They built companies that changed the world. If that's your goal, great; I wish you the best of luck. The Elon Musks, Jeff Bezoses, and Mark Zuckerbergs of the world made their fortunes living a lifestyle that combined an absolute obsession for their work, a spark of genius, and made a significant impact on the entire world in a way that most of us won't. They didn't make $100 million because they made some crazy trade in the stock market; they built multi-billion/trillion-dollar companies in which they maintained a sizable ownership stake.

For most of us, that isn't a practical goal. Most people have chosen careers where making tens of millions, let alone hundreds of millions of dollars, isn't possible.

If you approach the stock market imagining that you're going to make millions/billions while starting out at $100,000, that isn't practical. You can't always get what you want, and the stock market isn't a place to get rich quickly. If you set some insane goals that aren't achievable, you aren't likely to achieve any goal. You are going to give up because you aren't making any meaningful progress.

We want our goal to be specific. Something that you can look at every year and tell if you are moving closer or further away. As with any large project, the sooner you identify that you are falling behind, the easier it is to catch up.

We also want our goal to be practical. Something that can reasonably be achievable and can be broken into smaller bite-sized chunks.

Why Value Is a Poor Goal

The most common mistake I see among those looking to plan their retirement is that they set goals in terms of portfolio value. In the old days, it was always $1 million. It sounds like a lot of money, and it is. Yet today, many experts suggest that you need $2 million or $3 million to ensure that you have a comfortable retirement.

All this advice has one major problem: $1 million in stocks today is not the same as $1 million in stocks yesterday or tomorrow. Someone working on retiring in the 1990s might have been thrilled to hit their $1 million goal early and retire in January 2000.

However, over the next three years, the market fell, and their $1 million became just ~$600k. This is even before we account for any money they needed to withdraw:

This isn't an idle or theoretical risk. The stock market declines frequently, with 5-10% dips being very common. Dips of +40% are rare, but when they happen, it is usually many years before it goes back to all-time highs.

Yes, the stock market has trended upward over long periods, and it is a great generator of wealth for those who are willing to hang on through the swings. But for those who retired in 2000, thinking they had enough, seeing 40% of their retirement disappear in just three years is devastating. When you retire, you often can't go back to a position that has comparable pay to what you were getting before you retired.

And are you comfortable taking out money when the stock market is far down? We've discussed in recent articles the potential risks of withdrawing too much. The response of experts in the field has been to generally suggest a very conservative withdrawal strategy. Many fall back on the work of William Bengen, who suggested that 4% withdrawals from a portfolio of 60% equity and 40% bonds, adjusting for inflation, were sustainable in any scenario.

If you are looking to be certain, then yes, the 4% rule is going to create a portfolio that will survive almost anything. As a result, it's a very safe recommendation for those in the business to suggest because it does avoid the ultimate worst-case scenario of a retiree outliving their portfolio.

The problem with that rule is that in many cases, a retiree could withdraw a lot more and be just fine. The investor who retired at $1 million in 2003 can safely withdraw a lot more than the investor who retired at $1 million in 2000.

The challenge is to know how much you can withdraw in real time. The risk is that of living your life poorly, when you have built up a portfolio that is more than capable of supporting the lifestyle you want to live. Life is precious, and you only get to live it once. When you're sitting there at 100 years old, you don't want to be sitting on millions looking back at the life experiences you passed up on when you were younger and in better health—that is a risk. Maybe being completely broke is a worse scenario, but living in poverty to die wealthy is also a bad scenario.

The problem is that you can't look at the value of your stock portfolio and know whether it is going to be safe to withdraw 7% or only 4%, because it really matters whether you are retiring in 1997, 2000, or 2003. $1 million in asset value is dramatically different for each year in terms of its ability to fund your retirement for the next 30 years.

Set an Income Goal

Stock market valuations and prices change frequently. Interest and dividends change more rarely, and in a diversified portfolio, by much smaller amounts. Whether market prices are relatively high or low isn't particularly relevant to the income that a portfolio is generating.

You've spent your entire life working on a budget. You make $X in income. You made money from your job, maybe your spouse worked as well, maybe you owned a rental property, or other sources of income. You made money, and you spent less. It's a process we are all familiar with. And that money wasn't static. Who hasn't lost a job, had variable hours, or worked on commissions or ran a business, having extremely variable income?

Why do we suddenly treat retirement differently? You have the same bills coming in every month. The only thing that's changed is that you will no longer receive income from working. That income will be replaced by some combination of a pension, social security, and income derived from your retirement nest egg.

Many will sit there and tell you that you should maximize the value of your nest egg with the intent to sell it off in pieces, hoping that you don't live too long or sell such small pieces of it that even in a crashing market, it won't run out. If that idea fills you with dread, it's because it isn't natural and you have common sense. Selling assets to pay the bills is like the person who goes to the pawn shop to pay rent. It might solve the immediate problem, but that problem is going to come next month. Your bills don't stop, and those shares you sold are gone forever.

It's very difficult to know how big your nest egg has to be because you don't know how the market value is going to change. It could go up or down 40% next year. That's normal for the stock market, and normal things should be expected to happen. We shouldn't retire and cross our fingers, hoping that normal things don't happen.

How much income do you need to retire? Well, that's easy. You can just look at the income you want to replace. You are still going to live on less, and you are still going to reinvest a portion of it for the future. Just because you are retired doesn't mean life is over. You still have a future; you still need to invest in it!

We recommend planning on reinvesting at least 25% of your income. If you can reinvest more, that's great because it will help your portfolio's income grow faster and provide a larger cushion from disruption.

This provides you with a solid and tangible number to aim for. You know how much needs to be replaced, so you can track your path to get there.

Estimate Your CAGR

If your goal is to have $100,000/year in cash flow by the time you retire, and you are at $5,000/year now, that looks intimidating. However, if you are 40 and looking to retire by 65, you have 25 years to get there—so, you need to grow your cash flow at a pace of 12.7%/year. You can calculate this easily with a CAGR calculator. Source

You can then look to see what your trajectory should be to keep this pace:

So, your goal next year isn't to get your cash flow "as high as possible", it is to get it to a pace of ~$5,640/year. To the extent that you exceed that amount, you are on pace to exceed your $100k goal, allowing you to retire earlier or with more income.

If you fall behind because there are dividend cuts, investments don't work out well, or you weren't able to invest as much new money as you intended, you will know you are off pace immediately, and you can make relatively small changes.

You can use a calculator like this to figure out the pace you need to achieve your long-term goals. Put your current income, your target income, and how many years you want to achieve that goal within.

This will give you a tangible annual goal to target to ensure that you are on track to meet your 5, 10, or +20 year goals.

Ideas to Get You Started

So, you know how much income you need in 2026, but which picks to buy?

Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA), which yields 9.4%, is an ETF that invests in preferred equities. PFFA is an ETF that is actively managed and uses leverage to amplify returns. As a result, PFFA management is frequently identifying opportunities that can outperform.

PFFA has materially outperformed peers that focus on following the index. We strongly encourage you to build up a portfolio of preferred equity and bonds as a base for your income portfolio. Yet that is a task that takes time. PFFA can provide quick diversification while you take the time to do your due diligence on individual picks.

Realty Income (O) is a REIT (Real Estate Investment Trust) yielding 5.8% that has trademarked "The Monthly Dividend Company". It follows a business model of investing in triple-net leases, which make most property-level expenses the responsibility of the tenant. O has scaled up to become the largest company in its sector. O pays a dividend monthly and raises its dividend numerous times per year. It has increased its dividend every quarter since 1998. The raises often are not large, but they are frequent, and O highlights the power of compounding income.

Annaly Capital Management (NLY) is a 12.2% yielding mortgage REIT that invests in agency mortgage-backed securities, or MBS. Agency MBS is a unique asset class because the principal is guaranteed by agencies like Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac. NLY's business strategy is to invest in agency MBS on a leveraged basis, profiting from the difference between the yield on MBS and its borrowing rate tied to SOFR. NLY's business is very sensitive to interest rate changes but carries very little credit risk. As a result, NLY tends to outperform during periods when the stock market is crashing. In other words, it is countercyclical. Having a few countercyclical holdings can really help your portfolio during periods of economic turmoil.
